skill.name: remotion-render
skill.description: Render React and Remotion component code into MP4 videos through the inference.sh CLI and hosted remotion-render app.
skill.summary: Create MP4 videos from React and Remotion TSX components using inference.sh.
skill.author: toolshell

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many external command alerts, but they are documentation examples for the permitted infsh CLI and related installation commands, not hidden executable code. The meaningful risk is that the skill sends user-supplied TSX and optional props to inference.sh for hosted rendering, so users should avoid confidential code, secrets, and private media unless they accept that external service use.

medium_findings.title: Remote Rendering of User-Supplied TSX
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to submit React and Remotion TSX code to infsh/remotion-render through the inference.sh CLI. This is legitimate for the stated purpose, but it moves user code, props, and potential asset references to an external hosted renderer. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for network and external processing risk. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The documentation explicitly names inference.sh and shows infsh app run commands with TSX input; no hidden exfiltration or malicious endpoint was found.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented workflow clearly sends TSX to a named external service. The same context also supports a legitimate rendering purpose, so the risk is medium rather than high.

medium_findings.title: Broad External CLI Permission
medium_findings.description: The skill allows Bash(infsh *) and provides many infsh examples. This enables external command execution through the inference.sh CLI when the assistant follows the skill. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for external command capability, but the commands are explicit user-facing workflow steps rather than concealed malware. Confidence: 0.78. Confidence reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and examples are direct evidence of CLI use, but there is no evidence of shell metacharacter injection or unauthorized command construction.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly grants infsh command use. The risk depends on user approval and CLI behavior, and the skill does not define a dynamic command builder.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Alerts Are Documentation Formatting
low_findings.description: Most Ruby or shell backtick detections are Markdown inline code spans and fenced examples for parameters, imports, and command usage. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for Ruby backtick execution. Confidence: 0.93. Confidence reasoning: The flagged lines are within Markdown documentation and code examples, not a Ruby source file or executable script.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number context shows Markdown tables, inline code, and fenced examples. No Ruby execution context exists in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high static alerts at the front matter description and input schema appear to match words such as code or codec, not cryptographic APIs. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Confidence: 0.91. Confidence reasoning: No hash, cipher, MD5, SHA1, DES, or random-number cryptography implementation is present at the cited lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain metadata and a parameter table for Remotion inputs. I found no cryptographic operation in those locations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Expected Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to the service website, a hosted image, and official documentation links. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious networking, with a minor supply-chain and privacy consideration because the workflow depends on inference.sh. Confidence: 0.88. Confidence reasoning: The links are visible documentation references and align with the skill purpose, with no evidence of secret transmission in the Markdown itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are openly documented and relevant. The residual concern is dependency on an external service, not hidden network behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Service Invocation with User Content
dangerous_patterns.description: Pattern: infsh app run infsh/remotion-render receives user-supplied TSX through the input payload. This is the core feature, but it should be disclosed because code and props leave the local workspace. Confidence: 0.84. Confidence reasoning: Multiple examples show the exact external app invocation and input shape.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit in the examples and central to the skill. It is risky only when users include sensitive code, props, or assets.

content.user_title: Render Remotion Videos from React Code
content.value_statement: Teams often need short generated videos without building a full rendering pipeline. This skill turns Remotion TSX components into MP4 output through the inference.sh rendering service.
actual_capabilities: Runs the infsh remotion-render app with React and Remotion TSX input.
Renders MP4 videos from components that export a default React component.
Supports width, height, frames per second, duration, codec, composition ID, and props inputs.
Shows examples for fade-in text, counters, spring animation, props, and sequences.
Documents Python SDK usage and streaming progress output for longer renders.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI or SDK and an authenticated inference.sh account.
Sends TSX code and render inputs to an external hosted service.
Only documents imports from React and Remotion, not arbitrary package installation.
Render time, cost, and media retention depend on the inference.sh service.

use_cases.title: Generate Product Launch Clips
use_cases.description: Create short branded MP4 animations from reusable React components and product data.

use_cases.title: Render Data-Driven Visuals
use_cases.description: Turn counters, charts, and staged sequences into video assets for reports or dashboards.

use_cases.title: Prototype Motion Design
use_cases.description: Test Remotion animation ideas quickly before moving them into a larger video pipeline.

prompt_templates.title: Render a Basic Intro
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a simple Remotion component for a three second intro video, then render it with remotion-render at 1920 by 1080 and 30 fps.

prompt_templates.title: Add Props to a Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Remotion component that accepts title and subtitle props, then render an MP4 using remotion-render with my supplied text.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Timed Sequence
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a four scene Remotion sequence with fade transitions, then render it as a square MP4 at 60 fps.

prompt_templates.title: Render a Data-Driven Animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Remotion animation that visualizes this dataset as a timed progress sequence, include props for the values, and render the final MP4.

output_examples.input: A request for a three second fade-in title video at full HD resolution.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares a Remotion component, runs the remotion-render workflow, and returns the generated MP4 video link.

output_examples.input: A request for a square animated progress counter with custom label text.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates a prop-driven counter animation and returns a rendered square MP4 suitable for social posts.

output_examples.input: A request to monitor a longer render.
output_examples.output: The assistant uses the streaming workflow and reports render progress until the video output is available.

best_practices: Keep components self-contained and export one default component for rendering.
Use placeholder or public assets unless external service processing is acceptable.
Specify duration, resolution, and frame rate before rendering to avoid wasted jobs.
anti_patterns: Do not include API keys, private customer data, or unreleased source code in render props or TSX.
Do not assume local-only execution because rendering is performed through inference.sh.
Do not submit very large renders without first testing a short low-resolution version.

faq.question: Does this skill render videos locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the inference.sh CLI or SDK to run a hosted Remotion rendering app.

faq.question: What input code does it expect?
faq.answer: It expects React and Remotion TSX that exports a default component.

faq.question: Can I pass dynamic values into the video?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented input supports props that are passed to the component.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential projects?
faq.answer: Use caution. Code, props, and asset references may be sent to the external rendering service.

faq.question: What formats can it configure?
faq.answer: The skill documents settings for resolution, frames per second, duration, codec, and composition ID.
